paper.title: Improved constraints on Galactic Centre ejection of hypervelocity stars based on novel search method

extraction.summary: Paper identifies 600 HVS candidates using a novel radial-trajectory method in Gaia DR3, obtains follow-up spectroscopy for 196 stars, and confirms only the previously known S5-HVS1 as a genuine HVS. All 599 newly identified candidates are rejected. S5-HVS1 is included as a cited-from-literature candidate.

method_chain.summary: Gaia DR3 astrometry (RA, Dec, parallax, proper motions), photometry (G, G_BP, G_RP), and RUWE for sources with G < 20.
outputs: source positions, proper motions, parallaxes, photometry

source_refs.context: Gaia DR3 data used for positions and proper motions

method_chain.summary: Compute implied distance D_I assuming a purely radial trajectory from the Galactic Centre, using observed sky position and Gaia proper motion alone.

source_refs.context: Method for computing implied distance from radial trajectory assumption

method_chain.summary: Compute implied heliocentric radial velocity V_r,I and total implied velocity V_I from the radial trajectory assumption.

source_refs.context: Method for computing implied radial velocity and total velocity from radial trajectory assumption

method_chain.summary: Require implied parallax consistent with Gaia parallax within 2 sigma, photo-geometric distance consistency, RUWE < 1.4, HR diagram main-sequence proximity, extinction-corrected colour G_BP-G_RP < 0.5, and A_G < 1.5.

source_refs.context: Quality and photometric selections for HVS candidates

method_chain.summary: Select stars with total implied velocity V_I in [800, 3500] km/s, ensuring the radial-trajectory assumption is reasonable and stars would be unbound.

source_refs.context: Astrometric and kinematic selections requiring V_I in [800, 3500] km/s

method_chain.summary: Apply sky-coordinate selections using HEALPix to restrict to high-purity directions, and exclude stars near the LMC and SMC.

source_refs.context: Sky coordinate selections yield 600 HVS candidates

method_chain.summary: Ground-based spectroscopic follow-up with INT/IDS and NTT/EFOSC2 to measure barycentric radial velocities for 196 candidates. Additional five stars use literature RVs from SDSS DR14 or LAMOST DR8.

source_refs.context: Instrument setup and observed HVS candidates with radial velocity measurements

method_chain.summary: Compare implied radial velocity V_r,I with observed radial velocity. Only S5-HVS1 falls on the bisection, confirming it as an HVS. Other candidates show significant discrepancies and are rejected, though observational uncertainty and non-radial orbits prevent absolute exclusion.

source_refs.context: Only S5-HVS1 falls on the bisection, identifying it as an HVS

method_chain.summary: Simulated HVSs are integrated in the Galactic potential after ejection. Mock observations incorporate Gaia selection function, astrometric uncertainties, and photometric properties.

source_refs.context: Ejected stars integrated in the potential of the Galaxy with mock observations

method_chain.summary: Use detailed Hills-mechanism HVS ejection simulations (Speedystar) combined with the null detection of new HVSs to constrain the GC ejection rate and progenitor mass function.

source_refs.context: Constraints on HVS ejection rate and mass function from simulations
The selection steps up to this point leave us with only 600 HVS candidates, the previously identified S5-HVS1 being one of the top candidates

inclusion_assessment.summary: S5-HVS1 is the only star in the 600-candidate sample that the paper explicitly confirms as a genuine HVS from the Galactic Centre. It falls on the bisection between implied and observed radial velocity, has a measured radial velocity of ~995 km/s, and is described as the fastest Hills-mechanism HVS known. The paper does not explicitly label it as 'unbound', but the selection criterion (V_I > 800 km/s) was chosen to ensure stars would be unbound, and its extreme velocity strongly implies an unbound orbit.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Paper explicitly identifies S5-HVS1 as an HVS, confirms it with follow-up spectroscopy, and contrasts it with all other candidates which are rejected. The object's extreme radial velocity (~995 km/s) far exceeds the 800 km/s lower limit chosen to ensure unbound status.

source_refs.context: Only the previously identified S5-HVS1 falls on the bisection, clearly identifying it as an HVS
It is the only HVS that can be unambiguously traced back to the GC and is, by far, the fastest out of the stars that are suggested to originate from the Hills mechanism
Previously identified S5-HVS1 being one of the top candidates
Radial velocity measurement consistent with earlier finding, confirming HVS status

citation_context_refs.context: Citation of Koposov 2020 for S5-HVS1 discovery
Citation of Koposov 2020 in discussion of S5-HVS1

bibliography_refs.context: BibTeX entry for Koposov_2020 with full author list, title, journal, year, volume, pages, and DOI

radial_velocity.description: Heliocentric radial velocity of S5-HVS1 measured by this work using the INT wavelength range 3850-5240 Angstrom. Consistent with the earlier literature value of 1017 +/- 2.7 km/s.

source_refs.context: The radial velocity we measure for S5-HVS1 is 995 +/- 12 km/s

spectroscopy.description: Radial velocity measured from INT spectroscopy using wavelength range 3850-5240 Angstrom.

source_refs.context: Radial velocity measurement of S5-HVS1

origin_site.description: S5-HVS1 is assumed to have been ejected from the vicinity of Sgr A* via the Hills mechanism.

source_refs.context: The only HVS that can be unambiguously traced back to the GC
Throughout this work, we assume that S5-HVS1 has been ejected by the Hills mechanism

candidate_groups_considered.description: The paper identifies 600 HVS candidates in Gaia DR3 using a novel radial-trajectory method. These candidates are selected based on implied velocity [800, 3500] km/s, parallax consistency, HR diagram position, and sky-coordinate selections. Follow-up spectroscopy for 196 candidates (plus 5 with literature RVs) confirms only the previously known S5-HVS1 as a genuine HVS; all 599 newly identified candidates are rejected because their observed radial velocities do not match the implied radial velocities.
candidate_groups_considered.reason: The paper explicitly states that it 'was able to reject all newly identified candidates' based on follow-up radial velocity measurements. Only S5-HVS1, which was previously discovered in the literature, is confirmed as a genuine HVS in this work.

source_refs.context: Only the previously identified S5-HVS1 falls on the bisection, clearly identifying it as an HVS
Utilising ground-based follow-up observations, we were able to reject all newly identified candidates
